questions.question:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

Column I:
(a) Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm
(b) Kruskal's minimum spanning tree algorithm
(c) Biconnected components algorithm
(d) Floyd's shortest path algorithm

Column II:
(p) Greedy method
(q) Dynamic programming
(r) Divide and Conquer
(s) Depth-first search

A. a-r, b-p, c-s, d-q
B. a-p, b-r, c-q, d-s
C. a-q, b-p, c-r, d-s
D. a-r, b-q, c-p, d-s
questions.to_find: Correct matching of famous algorithms to their algorithm design paradigms

understand.plain: This question asks you to match four well-known algorithms each to one of four algorithm design strategies: Greedy, Dynamic Programming, Divide and Conquer, or Depth-First Search. Each algorithm is a classic example of exactly one paradigm.

keywords.explain: A technique that splits the problem into subproblems, solves them independently, and combines results.
keywords.example: Merge sort splits the array in half, sorts each half, then merges.

keywords.explain: Solves overlapping subproblems by storing results (memoization/tabulation) to avoid recomputation.
keywords.example: Floyd-Warshall builds shortest paths incrementally using previously computed distances.

keywords.explain: Makes the locally optimal choice at each step hoping to find a global optimum.
keywords.example: Kruskal's picks the smallest-weight edge that doesn't form a cycle.

keywords.explain: Graph traversal that explores as deep as possible before backtracking, used as the backbone of many graph algorithms.
keywords.example: Biconnected components algorithm uses DFS with a low[] array.

stroke-width="2"/><line x1="240" y1="142" x2="280" y2="142" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="240" y1="184" x2="280" y2="184" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two columns: algorithms on the left (Strassen, Kruskal, Biconnected, Floyd) matched by arrows to paradigms on the right (Divide and Conquer, Greedy, DFS, Dynamic Programming).

given.aim: Match each algorithm to its design paradigm.

terms.meaning: Multiplies two n x n matrices using 7 recursive multiplications instead of 8.
terms.example: Splits each matrix into four n/2 x n/2 submatrices — classic divide and conquer.

terms.meaning: Builds a minimum spanning tree by always picking the cheapest edge that doesn't create a cycle.
terms.example: Always greedily takes the next cheapest safe edge.

terms.meaning: Finds all biconnected components of a graph using DFS and low[] values.
terms.example: Tarjan's algorithm for biconnected components is DFS-based.

terms.meaning: Computes all-pairs shortest paths using a DP recurrence over intermediate vertices.
terms.example: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k]+dist[k][j]) for each k.

given.plan: Recall the standard algorithmic classification for each algorithm: Strassen = D&C, Kruskal = Greedy, Biconnected = DFS, Floyd = DP. Then map to the given labels.

steps.title: Classify each algorithm
steps.apply:
Strassen's matrix multiplication: recursively divides matrices into subproblems -> Divide and Conquer (r).
Kruskal's MST: always picks minimum weight edge not forming a cycle -> Greedy (p).
Biconnected components: uses DFS traversal with discovery/low times -> Depth-First Search (s).
Floyd's shortest path (Floyd-Warshall): dp[i][j][k] recurrence over all intermediate nodes -> Dynamic Programming (q).
steps.note: These are standard textbook classifications; each algorithm is the canonical example of its paradigm.

steps.title: Form the matching
steps.apply: a -> r (Divide and Conquer), b -> p (Greedy), c -> s (Depth-First Search), d -> q (Dynamic Programming). This corresponds to option A: a-r, b-p, c-s, d-q.
steps.note: Check each option against this classification to confirm A is correct.

questions.formula_note: Standard algorithm design paradigm classification: Strassen=Divide and Conquer, Kruskal=Greedy, Biconnected Components=DFS, Floyd-Warshall=Dynamic Programming.

questions.question:
Algorithm design technique used in quicksort algorithm is?

A. Dynamic programming
B. Backtracking
C. Divide and conquer
D. Greedy method
questions.to_find: The algorithm design paradigm used by Quicksort

understand.plain: Quicksort is a classic sorting algorithm. The question asks which design strategy it uses. Quicksort picks a pivot element, partitions the array around it, and then recursively sorts the two halves — this is the hallmark of divide and conquer.

keywords.explain: A sorting algorithm that picks a pivot, partitions elements less than and greater than the pivot, and recursively sorts each partition.
keywords.example: Array [3,1,4,1,5,9]: pick pivot 4, partition to [3,1,1] | [5,9], recurse on each part.

keywords.explain: An algorithm design technique that divides the problem into smaller subproblems, solves each independently, and combines the results.
keywords.example: Quicksort divides the array at the pivot, conquers each half, and the combination is trivial (already in place).

understand.visual_alt: Quicksort divides an array into two partitions around a pivot, then recursively sorts each partition — a tree of recursive calls forms the divide-and-conquer structure.

given.aim: Identify the design paradigm of Quicksort.

terms.meaning: An element chosen to partition the array into two parts.
terms.example: If pivot=5, elements < 5 go left, elements > 5 go right.
terms.connects: The partition step is the 'divide' phase.

terms.meaning: Rearranges elements so all elements smaller than pivot come before it and all larger come after.
terms.connects: After partition, pivot is in its correct position; subarrays are solved independently.

terms.meaning: Quicksort calls itself on the left and right partitions.
terms.example: quicksort(arr, low, pivot-1) and quicksort(arr, pivot+1, high).
terms.connects: The independent recursive solving of subproblems is the 'conquer' phase.

given.plan: Identify that Quicksort divides the problem (partition around pivot) and conquers subproblems recursively, matching Divide and Conquer paradigm.

steps.title: Recall Quicksort structure
steps.apply: Quicksort: (1) Select a pivot element. (2) Partition the array: elements < pivot to left, elements > pivot to right. (3) Recursively apply quicksort to left and right subarrays. This is exactly the Divide (partition) + Conquer (recursive sort) + Combine (trivial, already in place) pattern.
steps.note: The combine step in quicksort is O(1) — the subarrays are already in place after recursion.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Dynamic programming: requires overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure — quicksort subproblems do not overlap. Backtracking: involves exploring and undoing choices — quicksort does not backtrack. Greedy: makes a single locally optimal choice — quicksort makes recursive calls on both partitions, not a single greedy choice.
steps.note: Pivot selection could seem 'greedy' but the defining structure is recursive divide-and-conquer.

questions.formula_note: Quicksort uses Divide and Conquer: divide by partitioning around a pivot, conquer by recursively sorting each partition, combine trivially.

questions.question:
Merge sort uses:

A. Divide and conquer strategy
B. Backtracking approach
C. Heuristic search
D. Greedy approach
questions.to_find: The algorithm design strategy used by Merge Sort

understand.plain: Merge sort is a sorting algorithm that splits the array into two halves, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves. This splitting and recursive solving is the classic Divide and Conquer approach.

keywords.explain: A stable sorting algorithm that recursively divides the array into halves, sorts each half, and merges the sorted halves.
keywords.example: Sort [5,3,8,1]: divide into [5,3] and [8,1], sort each to [3,5] and [1,8], merge to [1,3,5,8].

keywords.explain: Algorithm paradigm: divide the problem into subproblems, solve independently, combine results.
keywords.example: Merge sort divides (split), conquers (recursively sort halves), and combines (merge step).

understand.visual_alt: Merge sort tree: the array [5,3,8,1] is divided into [5,3] and [8,1], each is sorted recursively to [3,5] and [1,8], then merged into [1,3,5,8].

given.aim: Identify the algorithm design strategy used by Merge Sort.

terms.meaning: Split the array into two halves of roughly equal size.
terms.example: Array of 8 elements -> two arrays of 4.
terms.connects: Reduces problem size; the 'divide' in divide and conquer.

terms.meaning: Recursively sort each half until base case (single element).
Merge two sorted halves into a single sorted array.
terms.example: Merge [3,5] and [1,8] into [1,3,5,8] in O(n) time.
terms.connects: The non-trivial 'combine' that makes merge sort correct.

given.plan: Recognize that Merge Sort has three distinct phases (divide/conquer/combine) which directly correspond to the Divide and Conquer paradigm.

steps.title: Identify the structure of Merge Sort
steps.apply: Merge sort: (1) If array has 1 element, return. (2) Split into two halves of size n/2. (3) Recursively sort left half, recursively sort right half. (4) Merge the two sorted halves in O(n) time. The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) is the signature of a divide-and-conquer algorithm splitting into 2 equal subproblems.
steps.note: This recurrence solves to O(n log n) by Master Theorem (Case 2).

steps.title: Confirm and eliminate other options
steps.apply: Backtracking: involves trial-and-error with undoing decisions — not present in merge sort. Heuristic search: uses problem-specific approximations to guide search — merge sort is exact and deterministic. Greedy: makes one locally optimal choice — merge sort makes recursive calls on both halves.
steps.note: Merge sort is the canonical textbook example of Divide and Conquer.

questions.formula_note: Merge Sort exemplifies Divide and Conquer: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), giving O(n log n) time complexity via the Master Theorem.

questions.question:
The correct matching for the following pairs is:

Column I:
A. All pairs shortest path
B. Quick Sort
C. Minimum weight spanning tree
D. Connected Components

Column II:
1. Greedy
2. Depth-First Search
3. Dynamic Programming
4. Divide and Conquer

A. A-2, B-4, C-1, D-3
B. A-3, B-4, C-1, D-2
C. A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1
D. A-4, B-1, C-2, D-3
questions.to_find: Correct matching of algorithms to their design paradigms

understand.plain: This question pairs four well-known problems/algorithms with the design paradigm used to solve them. All-pairs shortest path uses Floyd-Warshall (DP), Quick Sort uses divide and conquer, Minimum spanning tree uses Kruskal/Prim (Greedy), and finding connected components uses DFS.

keywords.explain: Finding shortest paths between every pair of vertices in a graph, solved by Floyd-Warshall using Dynamic Programming.
keywords.example: Floyd-Warshall: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k]+dist[k][j]) for each intermediate vertex k.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree of minimum total edge weight; Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms are Greedy.
keywords.example: Kruskal's picks cheapest edge that doesn't form a cycle — pure greedy selection.

understand.visual_alt: Matching table: All-pairs shortest path -> Dynamic Programming, Quick Sort -> Divide and Conquer, Minimum weight spanning tree -> Greedy, Connected Components -> DFS.

given.aim: Match each algorithm/problem to its design paradigm.

terms.meaning: DP algorithm that considers each vertex as an intermediate, builds up shortest paths.
terms.example: O(V^3) DP with recurrence: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k]+dist[k][j]).

terms.meaning: Partitions array around a pivot and recursively sorts subarrays.
terms.example: pivot splits [1..n] into two independent subproblems.

terms.meaning: Builds MST by always choosing the cheapest valid edge (Kruskal) or cheapest edge connecting tree to non-tree vertex (Prim).
terms.example: At each step, locally optimal edge is selected — greedy choice.

terms.meaning: Identifies all connected components in an undirected graph using DFS/BFS traversal.
terms.example: Run DFS from each unvisited node; each DFS tree root identifies one component.

given.plan: Classify each algorithm/problem by its paradigm: Floyd-Warshall=DP, Quicksort=D&C, MST=Greedy, Connected Components=DFS.

steps.title: Classify each algorithm
steps.apply:
A. All pairs shortest path: Floyd-Warshall algorithm uses DP recurrence over intermediate vertices -> Dynamic Programming (3).
B. Quick Sort: divides array at pivot, conquers left and right parts recursively -> Divide and Conquer (4).
C. Minimum weight spanning tree: Kruskal's/Prim's always pick the minimum weight edge greedily -> Greedy (1).
D. Connected Components: implemented via DFS, marking visited nodes to identify each component -> Depth-First Search (2).
steps.note: Each algorithm is the canonical example taught in textbooks for its paradigm.

steps.title: Map to answer option
steps.apply: A->3, B->4, C->1, D->2 matches option B: A-3, B-4, C-1, D-2.
steps.note: Verify by checking other options: A gives wrong D->3 and A->2; C gives wrong C->2 and D->1; D is entirely wrong.

questions.formula_note: All-pairs shortest path = Floyd-Warshall (DP); Quicksort = Divide and Conquer; MST = Greedy (Kruskal/Prim); Connected Components = DFS.

questions.question:
Which one of the following algorithm design techniques is used in finding all pairs of shortest distances in a graph?

A. Dynamic programming
B. Backtracking
C. Greedy
D. Divide and Conquer
questions.to_find: The algorithm design technique used to find all-pairs shortest distances in a graph

understand.plain: Finding shortest distances between all pairs of vertices in a graph is a classic problem solved by the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. Floyd-Warshall builds up solutions by considering each vertex as an intermediate vertex and using previously computed shorter paths — this is Dynamic Programming.

keywords.explain: Computing the shortest path between every pair of vertices (u, v) in a weighted graph.
keywords.example: In a graph with 4 vertices, find shortest path from 1 to 2, 1 to 3, 1 to 4, 2 to 3, etc.

keywords.explain: A Dynamic Programming algorithm that finds all-pairs shortest paths in O(V^3) by incrementally considering each vertex as an intermediate node.
keywords.example: dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]) for each intermediate vertex k from 1 to V.

keywords.explain: An algorithm design technique that breaks problems into overlapping subproblems and stores results to avoid recomputation.
keywords.example: Floyd-Warshall stores dist[i][j] for all i,j after considering k intermediate vertices — each step builds on previous results.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 460 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="460" height="200" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="230" y="26" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Floyd-Warshall: DP for All-Pairs Shortest Path</text><rect x="10" y="40" width="200" height="140" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="110" y="60" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">dist[i][j][k] = min(</text><text x="110" y="80" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">dist[i][j][k-1],</text><text x="110" y="100" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">dist[i][k][k-1] +</text><text x="110" y="120" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">dist[k][j][k-1]</text><text x="110" y="140" class="tag ink" text-anchor="middle">)</text><text x="110" y="165" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">k = intermediate vertex</text><rect x="240" y="40" width="210" height="140" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="345" y="65" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">For k = 1 to V:</text><text x="345" y="90" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">For all i, j:</text><text x="345" y="115" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">Update dist[i][j]</text><text x="345" y="140" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">if path through k</text><text x="345" y="165" class="sub ink" text-anchor="middle">is shorter</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Floyd-Warshall DP: for each intermediate vertex k, update dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]). Three nested loops over V vertices gives O(V^3) complexity.

given.aim: Identify which design technique is used to find all-pairs shortest paths in a graph.

terms.meaning: A shortest path from i to j via intermediate vertices {1..k} either uses vertex k or doesn't — both sub-cases are shortest paths themselves.
terms.example: If shortest path from A to C goes through B, then A->B and B->C are also shortest paths.

terms.meaning: The same dist[i][j] values are needed repeatedly across different computations.
terms.example: dist[1][3] might be needed when computing dist[1][5] and dist[2][5] both.
terms.connects: Justifies storing results in a 2D array — the DP table.

terms.example: Consider vertex k=3 as intermediate: if going i->3->j is shorter than current i->j, update.
terms.connects: The defining DP formula for all-pairs shortest paths.

given.plan: Recognize that Floyd-Warshall solves all-pairs shortest path using DP: it satisfies optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, and its recurrence is the classic DP formulation.

steps.title: Identify the standard algorithm for all-pairs shortest path
steps.apply: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm solves all-pairs shortest paths. It works by iterating over each vertex k as a potential intermediate vertex and updating shortest path estimates. This produces a solution in O(V^3) time using a V x V distance matrix.
steps.note: Dijkstra's algorithm finds single-source shortest paths (greedy), not all-pairs. Running Dijkstra V times gives all-pairs but that approach is still greedy; Floyd-Warshall is the standard all-pairs DP algorithm.

steps.title: Confirm Floyd-Warshall is Dynamic Programming
steps.apply: Floyd-Warshall satisfies both DP properties: (1) Optimal substructure — a shortest path using intermediate vertices {1..k} is built from shortest paths using {1..k-1}. (2) Overlapping subproblems — dist values are reused across multiple updates. The algorithm fills a DP table of size V x V, updating it V times.
steps.note: Backtracking would be too slow (exponential). Greedy doesn't work for all-pairs in general graphs with negative edges. Divide and conquer doesn't naturally apply here.

questions.formula_note: Floyd-Warshall algorithm uses Dynamic Programming with recurrence dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k]+dist[k][j]), running in O(V^3).
